standard_description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
For the class party, Robin and Shawn each made a loaf of banana bread. Their loaf pans were exactly the same size. Robin sliced her banana bread into 6 equal slices. Shawn also sliced his into 6 equal slices. After the party, Robin had more slices of banana bread left to take home than Shawn did. What fraction of the whole loaf pan might each person take home?
Problem 2
a.   Choose each statement that is true.
i.
$$\frac{3}{4}$$
is greater than
$$\frac{5}{4}$$
.
ii.
$$\frac{5}{4}$$
is greater than
$$\frac{3}{4}$$
.
iii.
$$\frac{3}{4}>\frac{5}{4}.$$
iv.
$$\frac{3}{4}<\frac{5}{4}.$$
v.
$$\frac{5}{4}>\frac{3}{4}.$$
vi.
$$\frac{5}{4}<\frac{3}{4}.$$
vii.  None of these.
b.
$$\frac{3}{4}$$
and
$$\frac{5}{4}$$
are shown on the number line. Which is correct?
i.
###IMAGE0###
ii.
###IMAGE1###
iii. Neither of these.

Problem 1
Fill in the blank to make the statement true.
$$\frac{3}{4}>\frac{3}{\square}$$
Problem 2
a.   Compare
$$5\over 6$$
and
$$5\over 8$$
. Use <, >, or = to record your comparison.
b.   Explain how you know your answer in Part A is correct. Draw a picture or a number line to support your reasoning.

Problem 1
Fill in the blank to make each equation true.
a.   4 feet = ______ inches
b.   3 yards 1 foot = ______ feet
Problem 2
Determine whether each statement below is true or false. If the statement is false, change the right side of the comparison to make it true.
a.   6 yards < 20 feet __________________
b.   7 feet 1 inch > 90 inches __________________

Problem 1
Play the following game with a partner using a set of cards (Template 1 or Template 2). The goal is to compare the two fractions appearing on each card, determining if they are equivalent and, if not, which is greater. Instructions for the activity are as follows:
Go through the following steps with the fraction cards:
Select a card.
Individually decide whether the fractions are equal and, if not, which is greater. Then show each other your choices.
If you both agree, take turns explaining your reasoning. If you disagree, discuss until you reach a consensus.
Repeat 1 through 3 with a new card.
After 10 rounds, record observations about what methods you used to compare the fractions.
Problem 2
a.   Place the following fractions on the number line.
$$\frac{4}{1},\ \frac{2}{4},\ \frac{1}{4},\ \frac{4}{2},\ \frac{2}{2}$$
###IMAGE0###
b.   Compare each of the following pairs of fractions. Record your answer with <, >, or =.
i.
$$\frac{2}{4}$$
and
$$\frac{2}{2}$$
ii.
$$\frac{4}{2}$$
and
$$\frac{2}{2}$$
iii.
$$\frac{4}{1}$$
and
$$4$$
iv.
$$\frac{2}{4}$$
and
$$\frac{4}{2}$$
c.   Order the fractions in Part (a) from least to greatest.
Problem 3
Which is closer to 1 on the number line,
$$\frac{4}{5}$$
or
$$\frac{5}{4}$$
? Explain.

Problem 1
Use a yardstick and a 12-inch ruler to answer the following questions.
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a yard and the length of a foot?
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a foot and the length of an inch?
Problem 2
Fill in the following conversion tables.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Janella wants to go on an amusement park ride. She sees this sign before she gets in line for it:
###IMAGE1###
Janella knows that she’s 1 yard 8 inches tall. Is she tall enough to go on the amusement ride?
Problem 4
Show students
this image
from a CNN article with the information below.
###TABLE0###
How much taller is the tallest man than the shortest man?

Arrange your fractions in order from least to greatest. Explain your answer.
a.
$$\frac{3}{8},\frac{3}{3},\frac{3}{4}$$
b.
$$\frac{4}{6},\frac{2}{6},\frac{7}{6}$$

Problem 1
a.
Partition the following area model into thirds. Then write a fraction to represent the whole.
###IMAGE0###
Partition the following area model into sixths. Then write a fraction to represent the whole.
###IMAGE1###
Partition the following area model into ninths. Then write a fraction to represent the whole.
###IMAGE2###
b.   What do you notice about the number of parts and the size of each part in each model in Part (a)? What do you wonder?
Problem 2
a.
Partition the following number line into wholes. Label each tick mark with a fraction.
###IMAGE3###
Partition the following number line into halves. Label each tick mark with a fraction.
###IMAGE4###
Partition the following number line into fourths. Label each tick mark with a fraction.
###IMAGE5###
Partition the following number line into eighths. Label each tick mark with a fraction.
###IMAGE6###
b.   What do you notice about the number of parts and the size of each part in each model in Part (a)? What do you wonder?

Problem 1
Use <, >, or = to compare.
a.   1 half _____ 1 third
b.
$$\frac{1}{8}$$
_____
$$\frac{1}{6}$$
Problem 2
Annie drank
$$1\over 3$$
of a small juice. Enrique drank
$$1\over 4$$
of a large juice. Who drank more? Explain your answer in words or pictures.

Problem 1
Fill in the blank to make the statement true.
$$\frac{\square}{6}<\frac{5}{6}$$
Problem 2
Compare
$$\frac{2}{4}$$
and
$$\frac{5}{4}$$
using <, >, or =. Draw a model to show your thinking.

Problem 1
Complete the conversion table.
Distance
###TABLE0###
Problem 2
Fill in the blank to make the equation true.
21 km 80 m = ___________ m
Problem 3
The female world record for high jump is 2 meters and 9 centimeters. How high is that in centimeters?
Two fractions have different numerators and denominators. Is it possible for the two fractions to be located at the same point on the number line? Why or why not?

Problem 1
Kiana bought two Fruit-by-the-Foot snacks to share with friends. She splits one of them into 3 equal-sized pieces and the other into 8 equal-sized pieces. If Kiana were sharing a piece of Fruit-by-the-Foot with you, which snack would you take a piece from, the 3-piece snack or the 8-piece snack? Explain why.
Problem 2
a.   Locate and label the points
$$\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{4}, $$
and
$$\frac{1}{5} $$
on the number line. You can use more than one number line if you wish.
###IMAGE0###
###IMAGE1###
###IMAGE2###
###IMAGE3###
b.   What do you notice? What do you wonder?
Problem 3
Bryce drew this picture:
###IMAGE4###
Then he said,
This shows that
$$\frac{\mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{4}}$$
is greater than
$$\frac{\mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{2}}$$
.
a.   What was his mistake? Draw a picture that shows why
$$ \frac{1}{2}$$
is greater than
$$\frac{1}{4}$$
.
b.   Which of these comparisons of
$$\frac{1}{4}$$
with
$$ \frac{1}{2}$$
are true?
i.
$$\frac{1}{4}>\frac{1}{2}$$
ii.
$$\frac{1}{4}<\frac{1}{2}$$
iii.
$$\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{2}$$
iv.
$$\frac{1}{2}>\frac{1}{4}$$
v.
$$\frac{1}{2}<\frac{1}{4}$$

Problem 1
LaTonya has 2 equal-sized candy bars. She cut the first candy bar into fourths after lunch and eats three pieces. Later, she cut the second candy bar into sixths after dinner and eats three pieces. Did LaTonya eat the same amount of the first candy bar and second candy bar? If not, did she eat more of the first candy bar or the second one? Show or explain how you know.
Problem 2
a.   Who correctly compares the numbers
$$\frac{2}{3}$$
and
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
?
i.  Ben said that
$$\frac{2}{3}$$
is greater than
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
.
ii.  Lee said that
$$\frac{2}{3}$$
is equal to
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
.
iii.  Mia said that
$$\frac{2}{3}$$
is less than
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
.
b.   Compare
$$\frac{2}{3}$$
and
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
using symbols:
$$\frac{2}{3}$$
_____
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
c.   Choose the two pictures that best compare
$$\frac{2}{3}$$
and
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
a.   Choose each statement that is true.
i.
$$\frac{9}{8}$$
is greater than
$$\frac{9}{4}$$
.
ii.
$$\frac{9}{4}$$
is greater than
$$\frac{9}{8}$$
.
iii.
$$\frac{9}{8}>\frac{9}{4}.$$
iv.
$$\frac{9}{8}<\frac{9}{4}.$$
v.
$$\frac{9}{4}>\frac{9}{8}$$
.
vi.
$$\frac{9}{4}<\frac{9}{8}.$$
vii.  None of these.
b.
$$\frac{9}{8}$$
and
$$\frac{9}{4} $$
are shown on the number line. Which is correct?
i.
###IMAGE1###
ii.
###IMAGE2###
iii. Neither of these.

Problem 1
Use a meter stick to answer the following questions.
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a millimeter and the length of a centimeter?
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a centimeter and the length of a meter?
What do you notice about the relationship between the length of a millimeter and the length of a meter?
Problem 2
Fill in the following conversion tables.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Mr. Duffy wants to buy a new pair of skis. Skis are sold based on their length in centimeters. If Mr. Duffy wants to buy a pair of skis that are his exact height, and he is 1 meter 83 centimeters tall, what length skis should Mr. Duffy buy?
Problem 4
Two sisters stand back to back to see how much taller the older sister is than the younger sister.
###IMAGE1###
The older sister is 1 m 30 cm tall. The younger sister is 24 cm 2 mm shorter than her. How tall is the younger sister?

khan_exercises.title: Measure length in different units
khan_exercises.description: Practice measuring items in 2 different units and compare the size of units.

items.question_content:
The length of a rectangle is measured in jumbo paper clips and in centimeters.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7ced82a8c127035ba0408eb485105e33a2af671b)

**The rectangle is [[☃ numeric-input 3]] jumbo paperclips long.**

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/625a0f3a201c3b42715aa056d1c0c466e261ce31)

**The rectangle is [[☃ numeric-input 4]] centimeters long.**
hints: Let's measure the length of the rectangle in jumbo paperclips and centimeters.

The rectangle starts at the beginning of $1$ jumbo paperclip and ends after $2$ jumbo paper clips.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c35462ccaa23ada221889f275f541a650857a92a)

The length of the rectangle is $2$ jumbo paperclips.

On the ruler, the rectangle starts at $0\text{  cm}$ and ends at $3\text{ cm}$. 

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/686c9df16f266ddfcc4b3122810a31d23276e2c4)

The length of the rectangle is $3$ centimeters.
The length of the rectangle is $2$ jumbo paperclips and $3$ centimeters.

items.question_content:
The length of a rectangle is measured in small paperclips and in centimeters.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6f49e9a9807f40b95d9785d6f0ff3d91cc52db6)

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c104228ce3ce5128aea1d65b478dd76b78efee32)

**Which unit of measure is longer?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Does it take more small paperclips or more centimeters to measure the length of the rectangle?
It takes more small paperclips to measure the rectangle than it takes centimeters.

Since we need more small paperclips to measure the length of the rectangle, one small  paperclip must  be shorter than one centimeter.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ac6426eca8d72f2ee5f535f34e95d735eb8ff64c)
**One centimeter** is the longer unit.
